title: Functional polymers for energy, sensing and biomedical ...

good morning
everyone thank you all for coming
welcome to the first seminar series of
this uh season let's say hello to
everyone on uh on Zoom hi um it's a
pleasure for me to introduce sirini
karayan who's in the material science

and then she moved as a postto to

left uh she's she's had a really great

um you can tell that she's a really

think from her CV that went around
you'll have seen all the different areas
she works in and I mean for us I think
it's it's nice because it shows how uh
science can be so broad across several
disciplines even though she's a
physicist and she gets a chemistry prize
and she does AFM as well and and she's
got a prize for AFM as well so all of
those things I think she's a very

semin in our series and I'm delighted

we really look forward to your talk
seeing
you Lona uh this is my this is my third
time here um and uh it's it's it's
always been a pleasure visiting the city
uh especially was just telling David
it's completely rained out uh in England
so so my family is really jealous and my
group members very jealous when I send
them a snapshot of what it's like out
here uh so really pleased to be here
I've already met a a couple of uh of
people uh David mentioned about my peter
day priz and I just uh came across
someone who was Peter day's student
which is amazing yes Marta exactly uh
which is which is amazing yes so thank
you ever so much for having me here um
I'm going to talk about functional
polymers for energy sensing and
biomedical applications um and uh I I
always like to start by kind of giving
you an idea of what the scientific focus
is uh in my group and I like to start
with this question that I usually throw
out to my first year Material Science
undergraduate students and that is how
strong is a bar of copper so the way I
do it is so I have a a class of 300
students uh and I have a few of these
copper bars they they all look identical
and I I look around and I throw the
questions like so how strong do you
think this is uh I look for the most
athletic looking person I can find in
the audience and say do you want to give
give this a shot and I give them one of
these copper bars and I have one for
myself and I say go on bend it and this
person tries really hard and they're
just not able it's just a slender bar
and they're just not able to do it and I
was like oh it looks like you're having
some trouble here let me show you and I
bend mine and it's super easy and then
everyone suddenly is like Oho what's
going on because on the face of it they
look identical but what is different of
course is their micr structure so if you
look uh closely if you if you look uh
sort of you know if you uh look at the
look what's inside these bars are
actually made up of lots of little tiny
copper grains you know crystallites of
copper and depending on the size the
number of grains and the number of grain
boundaries the strength of these copper
bars can be very different so in fact
the one that I had uh was actually an
annealed copper bar so the grains were
slightly larger fewer grain boundaries
so dislocations could move more freely
which is why I could bend it whereas the
poor person uh next to me was left with
one which had loads of grain boundaries
which made the material much stronger
and what is the difference really well
it's exactly what I said I analed my
piece of copper right so there was
something about the processing that
changed the micr structure which then
gave rise to these properties that um
that were so different so so that's been
the scientific focus of of what we do in
my group and that is trying to
understand this relationship between
structure and properties and using
processing as a way of manipulating
structur such that uh we can get the
kind of properties that uh we like and I
showed you a simple example with copper
but then you can extend this to all
sorts of materials in particular uh I'm
interested in functional materials when
I say functional it's quite a catch all
term really I I'm interested in
materials that are responsive where you
can you know apply some sort of whether
temperature field whatever um and and
see some response often the response
that I'm interested in is electrical
response because I like measuring things
as David mentioned I'm a physicist by
training uh I started off by by by
measuring samples either in the you know
using STM or or um good old uh transport
measurements and so I'm always
interested in the the electrical
response that these materials might have
so uh again broadly speaking the
materials that uh I'm I'm interested
have applications in energy sensing and
of late a lot of biomedical applications
what's common of course is the materials
we work with as I mentioned functional
materials um and we often work at the
nanoc scale because this is where we can
use our processing roots to manipulate
the structure but of course we need to
understand what the properties are at
the nanoc scale so we requ a lot of
nanoscale characterization so a lot of
AFM or scanning probe microscopy based
techniques um and at the end of the day
I'm interested in making devices so
there's the materials but then how do we
make them into devices uh and so for
that uh I use additive manufacturing
methods so a lot of um printing methods
so not just 3D printing but more
advanced um microscale additive
manufacturing techniques some of them
I'll I'll talk about um today so that's
sort of where the device engineering
comes uh into play just to give you a
just a brief glance uh at some of the
devices that we work with so uh in the
in the field of energy uh I do quite a
lot of work on mechanical energy
harvesting I'll talk about this in in
today's lecture also some work with
thermal energy harvesting uh you'll
notice that a lot of these are flexible
and stretchable because some of the
applications I'm interested in are are
either in in wearables or or you know
areas where uh rigid uh devices wouldn't
quite work uh in terms of sensing again
I'll I'll touch on some of these we've
been doing quite a lot of work on
microfluidic devices uh of late uh also
trying to see how functional materials
can interface with Biology so using um
for instance here p Electric materials
to study cells um some work on on
textiles making them smart making them
responsive uh I'll touch on that today
um all the way up to wearable sensors so
these are some some very recent work
from uh my group where uh we can we're
actually looking at U analytes or
electrolytes or metaboly directly in
sweat um using these sort of wearable
microfluidic devices uh and onto
biomedical devices we have gone all the
way to uh sensors for Orthopedic
implants to soft actuators for robotic
applications and I I'll just touch
briefly on these at the very end um and
uh like I said what's common to all of
these really is the fact that one we use
scanning Pro microscopy to understand
properties at the nanoscale uh and
additive manufacturing to go from
nanomaterials to devices um so with that
the bulk of today's talk I'm going to
focus on on applications mostly in
energy and sensing uh in my group we're
mostly interested in how can we convert
mechanical energy and thermal energy in
the environment uh into electrical
energy and just to give you an idea the
the the focus or the the end goal is to
be able to power Wireless sensors you
know so so sort of like the internet of
things kind of a a scenario where you
have loads of sensors in the environment
they could be embedded in the walls or
in infrastructure or they could be
implanted within your body uh normally
you know how would you power such
sensors you could use batteries but then
batteries need to be recharged or
replaced so there are limitations there
uh one way is to harvest energy from the
environment to uh Power these and so uh
mechanical and thermal to me are are
ubiquitous I often again ask this
question question to my students like
what are ambient energy sources you can
think of and everyone says the sun which
is absolutely right but um for a lot of
these applications and also if you live
in England it's not always the most
accessible or convenient um option so uh
anyway um so to go from mechanical to
electrical energy I make use of p
Electric or trib electric materials and
for thermal to electrical I make use of
therm electric materials uh today I'll
mostly focus on on p Electric and and
tribal electric materials um and again
you know I I don't need to to labor on
this too much but um the the idea is
that the the devices that I'm looking to
power are low power devices so we're
we're talking about sort of microwatts
to mwatts uh range uh and that is
feasible from the sort of energy that is
available in ambient vibrations which is
not actually uh that high uh and the
most convenient way to go from
mechanical energy uh in the ambient
environment to electrical energy uh at
least for me is to go uh is to use p
Electric materials so um if you're not
familiar with with p Electric materials
these are U materials which by virtue of
their crystal structure uh can produce
electricity when they are uh when you
apply a stress or strain so basically if
you squeeze the material or you you
strain it somehow uh there's a change in
polarization which results in uh an
electric potential developing across the
material and you can have an external
circuit and and cause a current to flow
uh the reverse occurs as well you can
apply a voltage across these these
crystals or these materials and they
will change shape and you see lots of
examples in you know everything from
ultrasound machine to you know to gas
lighters where you uh where we make use
of this uh effect and typically these
materials are ceramic in nature so most
P electrics you come across you know
maybe berum tighten it leads or chronium
tightened so lots of um oxides uh which
which show this effect um and I I guess
the the important thing to to note is
you know you need some sort of uh non
Centro symmetric you need a noncent
symmetric crystal structure for this to
work you need to have a dipole somewhere
present in your structure which when you
then you know try to move apart or
squeeze together will change the
polarization which is then what produces
this voltage across the material so the
the existence of a dipole is very
necessary but from a mechanical energy
harvesting point of view these Ceramics
are not necessarily the ideal candidate
because as you can imagine I'm talking
about something which is you know being
squeezed or or strained repeatedly over
and over again and Ceramics are you know
stiff mechanically they're prone to to
failure so this is where I came across
uh another class of P electrics namely
uh p Electric polymers um which also
exhibit this effect but usually well not
usually they are generally much poorer
counterparts to the ceramic so they're
they're so-called p Electric
coefficients are often lower but they
have all the advantages that I would
like for a mechanical energy harvesting
device namely you know they're flexible
they're Rob they're robust um you know
they don't contain lead which a lot of
the uh uh ceramics
the common ceramic p Electric Ceramics
do um so so lots of things going for
them and and so the the question is how
do we make better p Electric polymers
such that we can then use them in a
device um so I've picked four candidate
uh p Electric polymers these are the
ones we mostly work within the lab um
and as I go through I'll talk about some
of the kind of uh studies that we do
with uh scanning Pro microscopy um
because the the only way to to Really uh
enhance F electricity is to is to is to
process these materials such that you
can manipulate their structure at the
nanoscale because think about it you
know Ceramics you know as I said you
know P electricity is rooted in the
crystalline structure Ceramics yes you
know well- defined crystal structure
polymerous not so much um you know a lot
of these are semicrystalline and even
then if you think of a polymer you think
of you know a mesh of molecules even
though you might have an individual
dipole within the chain overall when you
have this Tangled you know mass of of of
chain chains uh the dipoles basically
all cancel out so that's really where
the where the the challenge lies so
let's start with uh pbdf or polyvine
fluoride which is a you know kind of
poster child for p Electric polymers um
so as I mentioned size matters because
when you go to smaller scales you can
control better the crystalline
properties and you can also control
better the alignment of these um chains
but so does phase so phase is very very
um important and let me explain what I
mean so here's pvdf the structure of
pvdf so you have a carbon backb and
you've got hydrogen and Florine atoms on
either side um it usually crystallizes
in the alpha phase which is the
thermodynamically stable phase and you
can kind of see that the hydrogen
Florine atoms are sort of randomly
distributed across the chain now there
is a dipole present in this structure uh
you have the more Electro negative you
have a dipole going from the more
Electro negative uh Florine to to
hydrogen so it's perpendicular to the
chain but in this random configuration
you can kind of see that the dipoles
basically cancel out so this phase is
not p Electric in order to get to induce
p Electric I need a sustained dipole
right I need a polarization in the chain
and uh and for that I need this uh beta
phase where you have all the hydrogen
and Florine atoms lined up nicely across
the chain so this is the all trans
configuration here I do have a a
polarization a net polarization in this
structure um but remember just having a
lot of beta phase isn't enough because
at the end of the day you want all of
these or as much of this pH to also
align uh once you have like a uh you
know when you have a lot of these chains
um together so going back to you know
what I said at the very beginning uh we
know that processing can be used to to
manipulate or to control phase and micr
structure and we do this um using a a
simple template wetting uh method in the
case of our PS electric polymers uh so
you start with a with a nanoporous
template so this is you know basically
these are actually commercially
available um anodized aluminium oxide
membranes which are Ed for filtration so
they have sort of nanosized uh pores um
and uh what we do is we either dissolve
the polymer or we melt it and then we
force it through the through the uh
porous template this could just be by by
capillary action or by adding some sort
of um pressure uh Once the polymer fills
the the pores then we either do a
controlled evaporation if it's from
solution or or a controlled
solidification if it's from the Melt um
until you're left with just the the
template with loads of these Nano is um
grown within it you can see that they're
all nicely aligned because they're grown
within this um Nano confined geometry so
it's it's quite a simple scalable
versatile technique uh and just to show
you so this is actually my first PhD
student Richard uh Wier and and we
showed that by growing these nanowires
so so first off there's some you know
nice sem images kind of showing you what
these nanowires look like in the crack
you can actually get rid of the template
you can dissolve it away to reveal just
the nanoir or you can isolate single
ones to do um you know to do more
measurements uh but the first surprise
really this was surrender is that um if
you look at the X-ray uh defraction
Spectrum so the blue line here shows the
starting material so the powder that we
we started with and as you would expect
we have different crystallinities Alpha
Beta there there others as well but when
we made nanowires of this using this
method we seem to mostly uh be able to
get our beta phase which like I said is
the phase that we're after so so we're
already on the right track here um the
other thing uh we observed is that you
could make devices out of this
straightaway so I was you know I was
Keen to see whether this works I was
like right brute force method let's put
some electrodes across this device so
here's our template with all the
nanowires we put some electrodes this
kind of what it looks like it's just a
very thin membrane packed with these
aligned nanowires uh and the first
experiment was just tap on it like just
you know apply a little pressure and see
whether you can see an electrical
response and you do you do actually get
a current and voltage um output of
course these experiments were done with
a Shaker I didn't have the student
tapping around all day uh so this is a
Shaker where can control like the
amplitude with which you're applying
pressure and the frequency as well um
and it works out you know works out
really well the the the what we found is
that if you look at the energy
conversion efficiency of this uh
material here it's about 11% and to put
that into perspective if you do the same
experiment with just a film of pvdf or
bulk pvdf that number is about half a
percent so this is already quite a large
um uh Improvement but the most
remarkable feature that we found is that
these um nanowires are self-poled so let
me explain what I mean by self- poing so
remember I said you know Ceramics
fantastic you know they they give you
great uh PE electric um uh the PE
electric properties but even within a
ceramic like if you have a a you know a
chunk of berium let say it's still made
up of individual grains and while each
grain has a dipole moment associate or
or some polarization associated with it
if they're all pointing in random
directions same problem with the polymer
you can squeeze all you like you're not
going to get anything out of it so what
you need is for all the dipoles to point
in a particular direction so then when
you apply pressure or you know strain or
whatever you change the overall
polarization in order to do this you
whack the material with a large electric
field it's called polling electrical
polling you apply a large electric field
forces all the dipoles along the or more
or less along the direction of the
electric field and then you have
yourself a PS electric
material same rules should apply for the
polymer you should have to apply a large
Elric fi and in fact for films people
need to do this except for polymers they
often break down before you can even
reach that sort of Co of field that you
need in order to get this alignment but
what we found is we didn't need to poll
so in this device these were as
fabricated nanoir um and they were ready
to go so this um so there was something
in this in this uh in this growth
process and we kind of re understand a
little bit of how that is happening um
where where essentially as these as the
as the polymer is infiltrating these uh
these pores they're getting stretched
along the direction of the axis of the
pore uh and this is actually another way
by which polymers are pulled they're
like physically drawn in order to give
them that alignment we believe that's
sort of what is happening um here and we
discovered this in in other Pi Electric
Systems as well so this is very uh this
nice review uh paper that we published a
few years ago um a couple of years ago
where we kind of uh show how the
polarization is induced uh usually
either by drawing or by polling you can
see large electric fields are required
so this is the case in case of pvdf uh
nylons polylectic acid um this
particular material is not ferroelectric
so you cannot actually pull it so the
only way to actually get inducement is
by drawing but we don't do any of this
um just the the method of of this
nanoconfined growth is enough um but we
need to understand these properties at
the nanoscale and for that we turn to
good old AFM based methods some of you
might already be familiar with um in
this case we use P response Force
microscopy where we use an AFM tip to
apply a field across a sample uh and if
it's a p Electric sample it will deform
because you're applying a field across
it and you can you can measure that that
deformation with your AFM you know the
optical um setup so that's how it it
works you apply an AC bias and see
whether the surface of your sample is is
sort of uh getting displaced at that
frequency the problem is as you can see
it's a sharp tip uh and I'm talking
about sof soft polymers they don't
really go uh also I'm working with Nano
wires or you know uh individual uh Nano
objects and the minute and this is a
contact mode method you you have to have
contact uh and you're sort of like
scraping this tip across this soft
sample uh so you end up either
displacing or destroying the the
material so it's it's quite tricky doing
pfm on on polymers um so along with uh
my uh so this is a y was a postt talk
working with me at the time he's now
faculty at the tech neon uh we came up
with this new new pfm technique for
non-destructive characterization I won't
spend too much time on this but just to
say that sometimes you you you have to
uh you know modify the experimental
setups in order to make them work for
you but in this case rather than
scanning the tip continuously in in
contact mode we use a sort of an
intermittent contact method um by which
we can apply a very high frequency AC
field just at the point of contact and
then we can sort sort of deconvolute the
the the response to work out what the um
p Electric response is so this is a very
powerful technique and it kind of opened
up the door um for us to be able to to
understand uh or or study these
materials better so just a quick example
of uh of what these look like so on the
left you see topography images of these
nanowires uh p pvdf nanowires and if you
look at the right these are um pfm uh
images so this is kind of showing you
the the p Electric response and you can
see that even you know just as they are
you see that there is already a sizable
p Electric effect you can enhance that
by actually using the tip to induce some
polling so you can apply a a DC bias
across what we did that around the
middle to show that you can induce the
polarization even further but just to
show that even without this um the
material is already inherently selfed uh
we we try to look at uh other processing
techniques and how this affects fear
electricity so this some work we did
with my good friend AA in Poland who's a
an expert in Electro spinning um so this
is a technique where you can make these
sort of fibers uh you know Nano to
Micron siiz fibers of you know pretty
much any polymer um by and the way it
works is you you you start with the
solution of the polymer uh through a
syringe and then you apply a very high
voltage often of the order of tens of
kilovolts between the syringe and a
collector um and uh the the sort of the
electrostatic repulsion once it
overcomes the surface tension at the at
the tip will will let out this jet of of
the polymer and you can use this
collector then to collect the this sort
of mat of um fibers U but there are
quite a few things that you can vary
it's it's a it's a processing technique
so you know you can vary the temperature
the humidity the even the the the sign
of the electric field so whether you're
applying a positive bias or or a
negative bias all of these have an
effect on the uh eventual properties of
the material and this is what we wanted
to find out so if you on the surface if
you just look at them you probably can't
tell much of a difference between say
when you deposited 30% or 60% but when
we start to use our our AFM based
techniques to look at p Electric
response so again at the top uh here are
a bunch of nanofiber or
quite largish fibers that are produced
under different conditions so the
numbers there represent the humidity so
that's 30% the signs there represent
whether we're applying a positive or
negative voltage so they look the
sameish um in in topography but when you
start looking at the pfm images you can
start to see some differences uh and
that's kind of summarized in this table
here so this is looking at pfm amplitude
for for these different uh fibers that
were produced under different conditions
and you can straight away see a winner
so it was the same starting solution
right the the pbdf you started off was
the same but then you end up with
conditions that give you much higher
responses um for one so this was uh
really very useful uh a really useful
technique to to try and understand how
processing affects the uh the property
so I showed you an example for pvdf
let's move on to some other less well
studied polymers so polyamides or nylons
also um have a dipole in their structure
uh particularly if they are an
odd-numbered um polyamide so by odd
numbered I mean that there's an odd
number of carbon atoms between the amide
and the and the co uh that that that
ensures that there's a dipole present in
the system if you have even numbers then
they sort of U they cancel out so if you
start with an odd-numbered Nyon so in
our case we we started with nylon uh 11
so this was work uh that was done by
Anuja who was a postu at the time she's
now faculty back in India um so she
wanted to see whether we could use this
nanoconfined growth process to enhance P
electricity in in nylon so it's the same
method uh you know of infiltration um
and then we this work was Then followed
by uh so yans was a PhD student in my
group he's now faculty back in uh in in
Korea in in yonsa University he spent a
lot of time trying to perfect uh or or
to understand the different stages so
you know the the the point of
infiltration the nucleation and
subsequently the growth of this material
within these um pores and how we could
manipulate this using various uh
external parameters like the temperature
of infiltration or or gas flow or uh you
know the the the the the the controlled
uh solidification process and all of
this is has been has been summarized in
this uh in this nice review article here
so I won't go into detail but the main
point I I want to make is that using
these techniques we can precisely
control both the phase which remember I
said was important and hence the p
Electric properties in nylon 11 uh and
just to say nylons have been known to
have p Electric properties they were
studied widely in the 80s and the '90s
but then they properties weren't as good
uh as C pvdf so you know we kind of died
out also they're hygroscopic so not very
um stable uh but we found that when you
grow them in this uh technique one you
can actually access phases that are not
accessible in thin film or bulk form for
example uh this so-called uh Delta Prime
phase so normally you have the this is
the thermodynamically stable Alpha phase
uh where you have these These Chains
that are very strongly uh hydrogen
bonded um so there is a dipole present
but there's not much you can do with it
they're they're quite
um they're actually very strongly in
place you cannot change the polarization
quite easily so this doesn't really give
you a a very strong p Electric response
whereas this Delta Prime phase which we
don't normally see it's a metastable
phase uh this has a a rotatable dipole
moment so we did some molecular
simulations to work out you know what
phases would likely give us the largest
polarization in the chain and and you
know this is the one that we were after
so uh y spent a lot of time trying to
get the right conditions in order to be
able to make this the prime phase again
you do not see this phase in the bulk or
in the films but only in nanowires and
then when we do some P response Force
microscopy look at the pfm amplitude um
as a function of of Applied voltage so
the higher the voltage the more the dis
the deformation you can see that the
again there's a clear winner here and
that's from our Delta Prime phase so
that's you know another nice example of
how you can um control P electricity in
these structures um but around the same
time we came across this uh phenomenon
you know well this it was making quite a
bit of Buzz and that's the the tribo
electric effect so this is an effect
that you are all familiar with we've
known about this for for you know uh
Millennia now uh and that's where you
have uh charge transfer that takes place
between two dissimilar materials when
you rub it's what we commonly you know
talk uh what we commonly refer to as
static and often you want to get rid of
static right you don't want that because
it it can destroy electrical components
but it is a way by which you can
actually convert sort of the friction
between two materials into you know
charge that is generated and then that
you can Harvest uh and use in a circuit
so for this to work ideally you want to
pick two materials which are uh you know
one which is very likely to give up
electrons one which is likely to accept
electrons so there are these empirical
tribal electric series that allows you
to choose uh so you've got more tribo
negative to more tribo positive
materials you can see lots of synthetic
polymers down here in the tribo negative
end but on the tribal positive end
mostly things like skin and hair which
is you know no good to make devices uh
with one notable exception and that
happens to be nylon when we were working
with nylon so we thought right let's see
if we can make this into a a tribal
electric material so in the case of a
tribal electric material there the
property that we're most interested in
is the surface charge right the higher
the surface potential the more the
surface charge the more likely it is to
ex you know to to uh generate this or to
um uh to transfer this uh during a a
rubbing event so in this case we use
what's called Kelvin probe Force
microscopy which allows us to map the
surface potential of these uh materials
so here are two uh examples so the the
top images are all topography uh and the
bottom images are all sort of looking at
the surface potential uh at the top of
these these nylon nanowires uh and in
the first image we just sort of measure
what the surface potential is in the
second image we actually zoom into a
smaller subsection of the image and we
use the AFM tip itself as the rubbing
element right so we use that to to
transfer some charge and then you could
zoom out and kind of see how much charge
you manage to transfer and you can
clearly see that there's a difference on
the left to the right and the main
difference is that on the on the left uh
we have water what we produced as uh
Delta Prime nanoes and on the right is
the alpha nanoes and you can see that
the alpha nanoir have a much larger uh
